revision.text:
[[en:Chiba Shrine]][[es:Santuario Chiba]][[ja:千葉神社]][[zh:千葉神社]] {{short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba, Chiba Prefecture|qq=}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Chiba Shrine
千葉神社 | image = File:Chiba-jinja 003.jpg | caption = Chiba Shrine's two-story shrine building (重層社殿, ''jūsō-shaden'') | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.611805555555556|N|140.12383333333335|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|qid=Q2842779|1=Ame-no-Minakanushi}} | founded_by = {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|en|Taira no Tadatsune|qid=Q1105844|lt=Taira no Tadatsune|1=Taira no Tadatsune}}, Kakusan | established = [[:en:1000 (year)|1000]] ({{ill|Chōhō (era)|en|Chōhō (era)|qid=Q1324844|lt=Chōhō|1=Chōhō (era)}} 2) | date_destroyed = | location = 1-16-1, Innai, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Chiba|en|Chūō-ku, Chiba|qid=Q1075869|lt=Chūō-ku|1=Chūō-ku, Chiba}}, {{ill|Chiba (city)|en|Chiba (city)|qid=Q170616|lt=Chiba|1=Chiba (city)}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|qid=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}} | website = {{URL|https://www.chibajinja.com/|qq=}} | architecture_style = | festival = Myōken Taisai (16-22 August)
Otori-sama (November) | leadership = | location = {{ill|Chuo|qid=Q1075869|1=Chūō-ku, Chiba}} }} '''Chiba Shrine''' (千葉神社, ''Chiba-jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Chiba|en|Chūō-ku, Chiba|qid=Q1075869|lt=Chūō-ku|1=Chūō-ku, Chiba}}, {{ill|Chiba (city)|en|Chiba (city)|qid=Q170616|lt=Chiba City|1=Chiba (city)}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|qid=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}. Originally a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} dedicated to the {{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|qid=Q4089509|lt=deity|1=Deva (Buddhism)}} {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|qid=Q10960623|lt=Myōken|1=Myōken}}, the patron of the [[Chiba clan]], it was converted into a Shinto shrine dedicated to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (a ''[[kami]]'' in [[Japanese mythology]] conflated with Myōken) during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}. Due to its historical status as one of the principal centers of Myōken worship in Chiba Prefecture associated with the Chiba clan, the shrine is also popularly known as '''Chiba Dai-Myōken''' (千葉大妙見, "Great Myōken [Shrine] of Chiba"), '''Myōken Hongū''' (妙見本宮, the ″Main Shrine (''Hongū'') of Myōken″), or simply as '''Myōken-[[:wikt:さま#Suffix|sama]]''' (妙見様). ==Deities== The shrine's main deity is the [[Kami|god]] [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] under the name 'Hokushin Myōken Sonjō-Ō' (北辰妙見尊星王, lit. "Venerable Star King Myōken of the {{ill|Polaris|en|Polaris|qid=Q12980|lt=North Star|1=Polaris}} (''Hokushin'')").{{cite web |title=御deity (Go-saijin) |url=https://www.chibajinja.com/about/gosaijin/index.html |website=Chiba Shrine Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-07 |language=Japanese|qq=}} The shrine's auxiliary deities are: *[[Futsunushi]] :Futsunushi (the warrior god of [[Katori Jingū|Katori Jingū]] in {{ill|Katori, Chiba|en|Katori, Chiba|qid=Q858357|lt=Katori|1=Katori, Chiba}}, Chiba) is jointly worshiped here as the site where Chiba Shrine stands was originally part of the precincts of a nearby Katori [[Shinto shrine#Famous shrines and shrine networks|branch shrine]] known as Innai Katori Shrine (院内香取神社, ''Innai Katori-jinja''). As the 'landlord' of the Chiba Shrine grounds, certain deferential customs are observed during the shrine's week-long Myōken Taisai festival out of respect for Futsunushi.{{cite web |title=シキタリ (Shikitari) |url=https://www.chibajinja.com/shinji/reisai/shikitari.html |website=Chiba Shrine Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-07|language=Japanese|qq=}}{{cite web |title=研究員の部屋 |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/kyodo/katsudo/kenkyuin.html |website=Chiba City Folk Museum |accessdate=2020-11-07 |language=Japanese|qq=}} *{{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} ==Background== {{see also|Myōken|qq=}} [[File:妙見菩薩（『仏像図彙』より）- Myōken Bosatsu (from the Butsuzōzui).jpg|thumb|left|190px|{{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|qid=Q10960623|lt=Myōken|1=Myōken}} (from the ''[[Butsuzōzui]]'')]] Chiba Shrine was originally a temple to the {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} {{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|qid=Q4089509|lt=divinity|1=Deva (Buddhism)}} {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|qid=Q10960623|lt=Myōken|1=Myōken}} ({{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}}: Sudṛṣṭi, lit. "Wondrous Vision"), the deification of the {{ill|Big Dipper|en|Big Dipper|qid=Q10460|lt=Big Dipper|1=Big Dipper}} and/or the {{ill|Polaris|en|Polaris|qid=Q12980|lt=northern pole star|1=Polaris}}. The cult of Myōken is thought to have developed in {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}} during the [[:en:Tang period|Tang period]], when [[Taoism|Taoist]] Big Dipper and pole [[:en:star worship|star worship]] was adopted into Buddhism.{{cite book |last1=Nakamura |first1=Kyoko Motomuchi |title=Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryoiki of the Monk Kyokai |date=2013 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1136792601 |page=85 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=EcjbAAAAQBAJ&pg=PA85|qq=}}{{cite book |editor1=Shively, Donald H.|editor2=McCullough, William H.|title=The Cambridge History of Japan, Volume 2: Heian Japan|date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |pages=549–550 |isbn=9780521223539|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eiTWWfoyuyAC&pg=PA549|qq=}}{{cite book |editor1-last=Orzech |editor1-first=Charles |editor2-last=Sørensen |editor2-first=Henrik |editor3-last=Payne |editor3-first=Richard |title=Esoteric Buddhism and the Tantras in East Asia |date=2011 |publisher=Brill |isbn=978-9004184916 |pages=238–239 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=F0XNX3N1a2AC&pg=PA238|qq=}} It was then introduced into Japan somewhere during the 7th century by immigrants (''{{ill|Toraijin|en|Toraijin|qid=Q11561926|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin}}'') from {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|qid=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}} and [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]. Myōken worship flourished in the eastern half of the country (the modern [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] and {{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|qid=Q129465|lt=Tōhoku regions|1=Tōhoku region}}) - where the ''toraijin'' were resettled during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|qid=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}} (reigned 661–672 CE){{cite web |title=妙見菩薩と妙見信仰 |url=https://www.ensenji.or.jp/contents/Category/believe/#post-1517 |website=梅松山円泉寺 |accessdate=2019-09-29|qq=}} - and was quite prevalent among many clans based in this area such as the [[Chiba clan|Chiba]] and the [[Sōma clan|Sōma]] clans. Temples and shrines to Myōken were especially numerous in former Chiba territories.{{cite web |title=千葉氏と北辰（妙見）信仰 |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/sogoseisaku/sogoseisaku/identitysuishin/chiba-myouken.html |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-07 |language=Japanese|qq=}} The relationship between Myōken and the Chiba clan is traditionally traced back to the clan's ancestor, [[:ja:平良文|Taira no Yoshifumi]], the uncle of {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|en|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|qid=Q1207652|lt=Taira no Masakado}}. Legend states that when Yoshifumi and his nephew was about to lose a battle against Yoshifumi's elder brother [[:ja:平国香|Kunika]] (father of {{ill|Taira no Sadamori|en|Taira no Sadamori|qid=Q3514057|lt=Taira no Sadamori|1=Taira no Sadamori}}) at the Someya River (染谷川, ''Someyagawa'') in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|qid=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province|1=Kōzuke Province}} (modern {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|qid=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}), they were rescued by Myōken, the deity of nearby Sokusai-ji (息災寺, modern [[:ja:妙見寺 (高崎市引間町)|Myōken-ji]] in {{ill|Takasaki|en|Takasaki|qid=Q336438|lt=Takasaki|1=Takasaki}}, Gunma). After reaching safety, Yoshifumi went to this temple to express his gratitude and took one of the seven statues of Myōken enshrined there with him. This image was passed down to his descendants, who venerated Myōken as their patron deity.{{cite book |last1=Faure |first1=Bernard |title=The Fluid Pantheon: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 1 |date=2015 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |pages=77–79|qq=}}{{cite web |title=千葉常胤生誕900th year 歴史文化フォーラム 議事録 |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/chiba-shi/kanren/summit/documents/summit02_11.pdf |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-08 |pages=3–6 |language=Japanese |date=2018-05-27|qq=}}{{cite web |title=平良文 |url=https://chibasi.net/souke3.htm |website=千葉一族 (Chiba Ichizoku) |accessdate=2020-11-08|qq=}} When the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|qid=Q1322181|lt=Meiji government|1=Government of Meiji Japan}} enforced the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] in the late 19th century, many shrines that venerated Buddhist figures or deities of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|mixed]] heritage either changed or associated their deities to ones found in classical [[Japanese mythology]] as written in texts such as the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Many places of worship dedicated to Myōken thus began to identify their deity as [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]], a [[Kotoamatsukami|primordial deity]] who (like Myōken) came to be identified with the pole star and the Big Dipper.{{cite web |last1=Mori |first1=Mizue |title=Amenominakanushi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=38 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |accessdate=2020-11-07|qq=}} ==History== According to tradition, the Buddhist temple '''Hokutosan Kongōju-ji''' (北斗山金剛授寺{{efn|''[[:wikt:北斗#Japanese|Hokuto]]'' (北斗) is the {{ill|Sino-Japanese vocabulary|en|Sino-Japanese vocabulary|qid=Q4222379|lt=Sino-Japanese|1=Sino-Japanese vocabulary}} name for the Big Dipper.|qq=}}) was founded in the [[Japanese calendar#Months|thirteenth day of the ninth month]] of the year 1000 ({{ill|Chōhō (era)|en|Chōhō (era)|qid=Q1324844|lt=Chōhō|1=Chōhō (era)}} 2) by the monk Kakusan (覚算), a son of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|en|Taira no Tadatsune|qid=Q1105844|lt=Taira no Tadatsune|1=Taira no Tadatsune}} (the paternal grandson of Yoshifumi), who then became its first abbot (大僧正 ''daisōjō'' or 座主 ''zasu''). The temple was built at the command of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|qid=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō|1=Emperor Ichijō}} (reigned 986-1011) in thanksgiving for his recovery from an eye disease{{efn|Myōken, whose name means "Wondrous Vision", is also invoked against eye ailments.{{cite web |title=妙見菩薩 (Myōken Bosatsu) |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A6%99%E8%A6%8B%E8%8F%A9%E8%96%A9-139760 |website=コトバンク (Kotobank) |publisher=The Asahi Shimbun Company / VOYAGE MARKETING |accessdate=2020-11-08|qq=}}|qq=}}.{{cite web |title=御由緒 |url=https://www.chibajinja.com/about/goyuisho/index.html |website=Chiba Shrine Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-08 |language=Japanese|qq=}}{{cite web |title=千葉神社 |url=http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/hisadome/honji/files/CHIBA.html |website=本地垂迹資料便覧 |accessdate=2020-11-08 |language=Japanese|qq=}} [[File:Chiba castle.jpg|thumb|Statue of Chiba Tsunetane with the {{ill|Inohana Castle|en|Inohana Castle|qid=Q28692626|lt=Chiba City Folk Museum|1=Inohana Castle}} (built on the former site of Inohana Castle) in the background|280x280px]] In 1126 ({{ill|Daiji (era)|en|Daiji (era)|qid=Q1037046|lt=Daiji|1=Daiji (era)}} 1), Tadatsune's great-great-grandson [[:en:Taira Tsuneshige|Taira no Tsuneshige]] transferred his clan's power base from [[:ja:大椎城|Ōjī Castle]] in {{ill|Kazusa Province|en|Kazusa Province|qid=Q1055394|lt=Kazusa Province|1=Kazusa Province}} (modern Ōji-chō, {{ill|Midori-ku, Chiba|en|Midori-ku, Chiba|qid=Q926492|lt=Midori-ku|1=Midori-ku, Chiba}}, Chiba City) to a {{ill|Inohana Castle|en|Inohana Castle|qid=Q28692626|lt=new castle|1=Inohana Castle}} located about a kilometer south of Kongōju-ji, situated in a natural plateau known as Mount Inohana (modern Inohana, Chūō-ku). During the transfer, the clan shrine to Myōken which enshrined the image supposedly brought by Yoshifumi from Sokusai-ji situated within the castle precincts was merged into the temple complex. Myōken Taisai (the "Great Festival of Myōken"), the temple's (and later, the shrine's) annual festival, was first held in 1127, the year after the clan - now calling itself 'Chiba' - moved to Inohana Castle, and has continued uninterrupted for nearly nine centuries since. As the Chiba's seat of power, the area surrounding the castle and the temple, Chiba [[Shōen|Manor]] ([[:ja:千葉荘|千葉荘]], ''Chiba-no-shō''), began to prosper during this period. During the {{ill|Genpei War|en|Genpei War|qid=Q737542|lt=Genpei War|1=Genpei War}} (1180–1185), the Chiba clan, headed by Tsuneshige's son [[:ja:千葉常胤|Tsunetane]], chose to side with [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] against [[Taira no Kiyomori]]. Yoritomo himself is said to have visited the temple on his way to {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|qid=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}} in 1180.{{cite web |title=千葉氏ゆかりの史跡・文化財 |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/chiba-shi/yukari/shiseki-bunkazai.html |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-08 |language=Japanese|qq=}} After Kiyomori's defeat, the clan was rewarded large domains throughout Japan. Myōken's cult spread to these areas as a result. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, a series of external and internal conflicts had severely weakened the Chiba. In 1455, [[:ja:馬加康胤|Makuwari (Chiba) Yasutane]] (son of 14th clan head [[:ja:千葉満胤|Mitsutane]] and brother of 15th head [[:ja:千葉兼胤|Kanetane]]) overthrew the clan's main branch, then led by his nephew [[:ja:千葉胤直|Tanenao]], and assumed leadership, only to be defeated and killed under the orders of ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|qid=Q447034|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimasa|1=Ashikaga Yoshimasa}}. Inohana Castle was abandoned in the aftermath of this conflict, with the Chiba (now under Yasutane's son [[:ja:千葉輔胤|Suketane]]) moving this time to {{ill|Moto Sakura Castle|en|Moto Sakura Castle|qid=Q11519850|lt=another castle|1=Moto Sakura Castle}} located in what is now the {{ill|Sakura, Chiba|en|Sakura, Chiba|qid=Q498011|lt=city of Sakura|1=Sakura, Chiba}}.{{cite web |title=岩橋輔胤 |url=https://chibasi.net/souke27.htm |website=千葉一族 (Chiba Ichizoku) |accessdate=2020-11-10 |language=Japanese|qq=}} The clan head's {{ill|Coming of Age Day|en|Coming of Age Day|qid=Q483226|lt=coming-of-age ceremony|1=Coming of Age Day}}, however, was still held at Kongōju-ji, showing the continued esteem with which the temple was held. [[File:Chiba-jinja 007.jpg|thumb|280x280px|left|The shrine's former (1954) ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', now an auxiliary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Tenjin (kami)|en|Tenjin (kami)|qid=Q1753428|lt=Tenjin|1=Tenjin (kami)}}]]In 1591 ({{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qid=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 19), Kongōju-ji received donations of land from {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, who also conferred to it the special privilege of direct audience with the ''[[Tokugawa shogunate|shōgun]]'' (normally a prerogative of the ''shōgun'''s direct vassals, the ''{{ill|Fudai daimyō|en|Fudai daimyō|qid=Q643640|lt=fudai|1=Fudai daimyō}} [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' and the ''{{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|qid=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto|1=Hatamoto}}''). The temple subsequently became known as 'Myōken-ji' (妙見寺) during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. In 1869 ([[:en:Meiji 2|Meiji 2]]), as a result of the edicts that called for the separation of Buddhism and Shinto, the temple - which featured [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|a combination of both]] - was turned into a purely Shinto institution and renamed "Chiba Shrine", with its deity Myōken reidentified as Ame-no-Minakanushi. In 1874 (Meiji 7), Chiba Shrine was raised to the Rank of [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines#Prefectural shrines|prefectural shrine]] or ''kensha'' (県社). In the same year, the shrine caught fire and burned to the ground. It was immediately rebuilt, but was again destroyed by fire in 1904 (Meiji 37).{{cite book |editor1-last=Furukawa |editor1-first=Kunisaburō |title=千葉街案内 (Chiba machi annai) |date=1911 |publisher=Tadaya Shoten |page=36 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/764037/26|qq=}} Reconstruction of the ruined edifices was finished ten years later, in 1914 ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|qid=Q329174|lt=Taishō era|1=Taishō era}} 4).{{cite book |editor=Chiba Prefecture |title=千葉県誌 巻上 (Chiba-ken shi, vol. 1) |date=1919 |publisher=Tadaya Shoten |pages=496–497 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/960652/297|qq=}} The shrine was destroyed a third time during the {{ill|Bombing of Chiba in World War II|en|Bombing of Chiba in World War II|qid=Q4940693|lt=bombing of Chiba|1=Bombing of Chiba in World War II}} in 6-7 July, 1945 ([[:en:Shōwa (1926–1989)|Shōwa]] 20) in the closing stages of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}. It was rebuilt after the war and was completed in 1954 (Shōwa 29).{{cite web |title=境内図 |url=https://www.chibajinja.com/about/keidai/index.html |website=Chiba Shrine Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-11 |language=Japanese|qq=}} In 1990 ([[:en:Heisei|Heisei]] 2), the entire Chiba Shrine complex was renovated on a grand scale. The ''Sonjōden'' (尊星殿), the shrine compound's {{ill|Nijūmon|en|Nijūmon|qid=Q3341490|lt=main gate|1=Nijūmon}}, was completed in 1998 (Heisei 10). ==Structures== [[File:ChibaJinja20100612.jpg|thumb|290px|The current ''haiden'']] *'''Main shrine building''' :The two-story ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' - the first of its kind in Japan - was built in 1990, replacing the previous one dating from 1954. Both the ground floor, the ''Kongōden'' (金剛殿, "Hall of the {{ill|Vajra|en|Vajra|qid=Q695709|lt=Vajra|1=Vajra}}"), and the upper floor, the ''Hokutoden'' (北斗殿, "Hall of the {{ill|Big Dipper|en|Big Dipper|qid=Q10460|lt=Big Dipper|1=Big Dipper}}"){{efn|The two floors are named after the shrine's former name as a Buddhist temple: '''''Hokuto'''san '''Kongō'''ju-ji''.|qq=}}, are open for public worship. *'''Chiba Tenjin (千葉天神)''' :An [[Setsumatsusha|auxiliary shrine]] dedicated to {{ill|Tenjin (kami)|en|Tenjin (kami)|qid=Q1753428|lt=Tenjin|1=Tenjin (kami)}}, the deification of {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} scholar and statesman {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|qid=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} revered as the patron of learning and scholarship, originally established within the Kongōju-ji complex in 1182 ({{ill|Juei|en|Juei|qid=Q598057|lt=Juei|1=Juei}} 1). During the 1990 renovation, the 1954 ''haiden'' was relocated and reused as a shrine building for Tenjin. *'''''Sonjōden'' (尊星殿, "Hall of the Venerable Star (King)")''' :The shrine's main entrance, a two-story gate (''{{ill|Nijūmon|en|Nijūmon|qid=Q3341490|lt=Nijūmon|1=Nijūmon}}'') doubling as a sub-shrine to Ame-no-Minakanushi (Myōken) in his role as the presiding deity of the sun, moon and stars. On the first floor is the ''Fukutokuden'' (福徳殿, "Hall of Merit"), an octagonal shrine representing the [[:en:Eight Trigrams|Eight Trigrams]] (Bagua) and the {{ill|Earthly Branches|en|Earthly Branches|qid=Q1072961|lt=twelve signs|1=Earthly Branches}} of the {{ill|Chinese zodiac|en|Chinese zodiac|qid=Q1976204|lt=Chinese zodiac|1=Chinese zodiac}}. The upper floor, the ''Kaiunden'' (開運殿, "Hall of Luck Improvement") is held to be especially imbued with the power of the deity and is only open a few times a year to devotees who request for formal prayer rituals (''kitō'') to be conducted there. Flanking the gate on either side are the ''Gettenrō'' (月天楼, "Tower of the Moon") and the ''Nittenrō'' (日天楼, "Tower of the Sun"). {|style="margin: 0 auto;" | [[File:Chiba-jinja Tor 08.jpg|thumb|The ''Sonjōden'' as seen from the second floor of the main building]] | [[File:Chiba-jinja_Tor_13.jpg|thumb|The ''Fukutokuden'', the eight sides of which represent the Chinese {{ill|Bagua|en|Bagua|qid=Q340846|lt=Bagua|1=Bagua}}]] | [[File:Chibajinja.jpg|thumb|The ''Sonjōden'' as seen from the shrine exterior]] |} *'''''{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|qid=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}}''''' (手水舎) [[File:Chiba-jinja 008.jpg|thumb|280px|The shrine's ''{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|qid=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}}'']] :This pavilion, dating from the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, was the only structure that survived the bombing of Chiba in 1945. The stone basin (''{{ill|Chōzubachi|en|Chōzubachi|qid=Q2972160|lt=Chōzubachi|1=Chōzubachi}}'') inside it dates from 1755 ({{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|qid=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki|1=Hōreki}} 5). The ''chōzuya'''s roof tiles were replaced in 2012 with exact replicas of the originals. ===Auxiliary shrines=== Chiba Shrine's auxiliary shrines (''massha'') are as follows: *'''Innai Katori Shrine (院内香取神社, ''Innai Katori-jinja'')''' :An auxiliary shrine to the warrior god [[Futsunushi]] located southeast of the Chiba Shrine compound, established in 885 ({{ill|Ninna|en|Ninna|qid=Q1346317|lt=Ninna|1=Ninna}} 1). The surrounding area, which includes the spot where Chiba Shrine stands, is traditionally considered to be the territory of this shrine. During the Myōken Taisai festival, Chiba Shrine's sacred palanquin (''[[mikoshi]]'') is brought to this auxiliary shrine before and after it is paraded across town, with the brass {{ill|Fenghuang|en|Fenghuang|qid=Q527525|lt=phoenix|1=Fenghuang}} that adorns the ''mikoshi'''s roof being attached or removed here - an act of deferential respect to Futsunushi. *'''Chiba''' '''Tenjin (see above)''' * '''Uba Shrine (姥神社, ''Uba-jinja'')''' :Dedicated to Myōken's {{ill|wet nurse|en|wet nurse|qid=Q472898|lt=wet nurse|1=Wet nurse}} (乳母, ''uba''). Reputed to date from the founding of Kongōju-ji, making it one of the oldest auxiliary shrines in the shrine complex. * '''Hoshi Shrine (星神社, ''Hoshi-jinja'')''' :Dedicated to the star god [[:en:Amatsumikaboshi|Ame-no-Kagaseo]], established in 1182. Medieval and early modern documents refer to sanctuaries dedicated to the {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|en|Twenty-Eight Mansions|qid=Q1378760|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions|1=Twenty-Eight Mansions}} (二十八宿堂, ''Nijūhasshuku-dō'') and the [[Decan#East Asian zodiac|Thirty-Six Animals]] (三十六禽堂, ''Sanjūrokkin-dō'') within the Kongōju-ji temple complex.{{cite book |title=房総叢書 第9巻 (Bōsō Sōsho, vol. 9) |date=1942 |publisher=Bōsō Sōsho Kankōkai |page=218 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1038196/116 |chapter=千葉臼井家譜 (''Chiba Usui Kafu'')|qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Wada |first1=Moemon |title=社寺よりみた千葉の歴史 (Shaji yori mita Chiba no rekishi) |date=1984 |publisher=Chiba Kyōiku Iinkai |editor=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai|page=18|qq=}} [[File:Chiba-jinja 006.jpg|thumb|300px|Auxiliary shrines (''[[Setsumatsusha|massha]]'') within the shrine compound]] * '''Ishi Shrine (石神社, ''Ishi-jinja'')''' :Dedicated to Iwanagahime, the elder sister of [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Konohana-Sakuyahime]], the goddess of {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|qid=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}. Originally known as 'Ishigami' (石神) or 'Ishigami Daimyōjin' (石神大明神). * '''{{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|qid=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine|1=Inari shrine}} (稲荷神社, ''Inari-jinja'')''' :Dedicated to the {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|qid=Q719665|lt=Inari|1=Inari Ōkami}} deities [[Ukanomitama]], {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|qid=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi|1=Ukemochi}} ([[:en:Ōgetsuhime|Ōgetsuhime]]), and [[:ja:ワクムスビ|Wakumusubi]], established in 1179. * '''[[Kotohira-gū|Kotohira Shrine]] (金刀比羅宮)''' :Dedicated to the gods [[:ja:金山彦神|Kanayamahiko]] and [[Ōmononushi]], established in 1840. * '''Nishinomiya Shrine (西之宮)''' :A branch shrine of [[Nishinomiya Shrine]] in {{ill|Nishinomiya|en|Nishinomiya|qid=Q243853|lt=Nishinomiya|1=Nishinomiya}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, established in 1782. Enshrines [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] ({{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|qid=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}). * '''Hachiman Shrine (八幡神社, ''Hachiman-jinja'')''' :Established in 1181 by Chiba Tsunetane, who enshrined a ''[[Shinto shrine#Re-enshrinement|bunrei]]'' of [[Hachiman]] (the patron deity of the [[Minamoto clan]]) obtained from [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|qid=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}} within the temple compound. * '''{{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|qid=Q656451|lt=Hie Shrine|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}} (日枝神社, ''Hie-jinja'')''' :Dedicated to {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|qid=Q10935105|lt=Oyamakui|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}, established in 1182. Originally known as 'Sannō Gongen' (山王権現). * '''Mitsumine Shrine (三峰神社, ''Mitsumine-jinja'')''' :Dedicated to {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}, established in 1822. * '''Shinmei Shrine (神明社, ''Shinmei-sha'')''' :A shrine to [[Amaterasu]] originally located in Yokomachi (part of modern Chūō-ku) and transferred to Chiba Shrine in 1882. * '''{{ill|Mount Ontake|en|Mount Ontake|qid=Q1754806|lt=Ontake Shrine|1=Mount Ontake}} (御嶽神社, ''Ontake-jinja'')''' :This shrine, established in 1871 and dedicated to [[Kuni-no-Tokotachi]], [[Ōyamatsumi]], and [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]], is in the shape of a mountain topped with three statues representing its deities. * '''[[Itsukushima Shrine]] (厳島神社, ''Itsukushima-jinja'')''' :Dedicated to the goddess [[:ja:イチキシマヒメ|Ichikishima-hime]] (originally [[Benzaiten]]), established in 1182. * '''Mizu-no-Miya (美寿之宮, lit. "Shrine of Beautiful Longevity")''' :The most recent of the shrine's ''massha'', established in 2009. Dedicated to the water deity [[Suijin|Mizu-no-Mioya-no-Ōkami]] (水御祖大神, identified with the dragon deity [[Ryūjin]]). The name ''Mizu'' (美寿 lit. "beauty [and] longevity" or "beautiful longevity") is a pun on the Japanese word for water, ''mizu''. In addition to the above, historical records also refer to shrines to [[:ja:清瀧権現|Seiryū Daigongen]] (清瀧大権現, the patron goddess of [[Daigo-ji]] in {{ill|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|en|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1197771|lt=Fushimi-ku|1=Fushimi-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}) and {{ill|Marici (Buddhism)|en|Marici (Buddhism)|qid=Q3273446|lt=Marīcī|1=Marici (Buddhism)}} (摩利支天 ''Marishiten''), as well as a sanctuary dedicated to seven important personages related to the Chiba clan{{efn|The four Taira ancestors of the Chiba ([[:ja:平良文|Yoshifumi]], {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|en|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|qid=Q1207652|lt=Masakado}}, [[:ja:平忠頼|Tadayori]], and {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|en|Taira no Tadatsune|qid=Q1105844|lt=Tadatsune|1=Taira no Tadatsune}}), Aihara Tsunetoki (粟飯原常時, a descendant of Yoshifumi's brother [[:ja:平良兼|Yoshikane]] - claimed to be the one who carried the statue of Myōken from Sokusai-ji - who was appointed Kongōju-ji's [[Kannushi|shrine priest]] in 1126{{cite web |title=粟飯原氏 (Aihara-shi) |url=http://chibasi.net/aihara.htm |website=千葉一族 (Chiba Ichizoku) |accessdate=2020-11-14 |language=Japanese|qq=}}), and Tsunetoki's two daughters.|qq=}} known as 'Sōdai-Shichisha Daimyōjin' (惣代七社大明神) within the temple complex. ==Festivals== *'''Myōken Taisai (妙見大祭 "Great Festival of Myōken", 16-22 August)''' :The shrine's annual main festival (''[[:wikt:例祭|reisai]]'') held without interruption since 1127. During the festival, the shrine's ''[[mikoshi]]'', called the ''Hōren'' (鳳輦, "phoenix palanquin"), is carried through the neighboring streets and deposited in a temporary shrine (御仮屋 ''okariya'' or ''mikariya'') near the former site of Inohana Castle, where it will stay for a week before being brought back to the shrine. Also known as 'Hitokoto Myōken Taisai' (一言妙見大祭), after a belief that any 'single word' (''hitokoto'', i.e. succinct) wish will be granted during the festival season.{{cite web |title=例祭 妙見大祭 (Reisai Myōken Taisai) |url=https://www.chibajinja.com/shinji/reisai/index.html |website=Chiba Shrine Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-15|qq=}} Both the shrine and the festival have been designated as cultural assets by Chiba Prefecture.{{cite web |title=Chiba Cultural Assets |url=https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kkbunka/olypara-c/chibabunkashisan-top-english.html |website=Chiba Prefectural Government |accessdate=2020-11-15|qq=}} ==Notes== {{notelist|qq=}} ==References== {{reflist|2|qq=}} ==See also== *{{ill|Astrotheology|en|Astrotheology|qid=Q123735772|lt=Astrotheology|1=Astrotheology}} *[[Chiba clan]] *{{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|qid=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province|1=Shimōsa Province}} *[[Taira clan]] *[[Towatari Shrine]] ==External links== *[https://www.chibajinja.com/ Official website of Chiba Shrine] (Japanese) <{{Myōken Faith|qq=}} {{Iwanaga Faith|qq=}} [[en:Chiba Shrine]] {{Commons category|Chiba-jinja}} [[es:Santuario Chiba]] [[ja:千葉神社]] [[zh:千葉神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11406700}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba]] [[Category:Buildings in Chuo ward]] [[Category:History of Chuo ward]] [[Category:Chiba clan]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages with 50+ untranslated japanese characters]]
